content.text:
<process_agent>:
Please open the file HydroWASTE_v10_-_UTF8.csv and provide information so I can determine what is in the file. For each sheet in the file, please provide the sheet name (if applicable), all column headings, the first 10 rows of data, the bottom 10 rows of data, the result of the Pandas describe function (include='all'), the result of the Pandas info function (verbose=true) and the result of the Pandas shape function. For all columns containing text values **(excluding numeric values, dates and any column with 'date' in the name)** also list out the first 100 unique values in those columns with single quotes around each. Place the results of all of these in a file datadescription.md.
Please use this code initially. If there are issues with execution, fix the code and run it again.
```python
# filename: datadescription.py
import pandas as pd
import io

file_name = "HydroWASTE_v10_-_UTF8.csv"

ext = file_name.split('.')[-1]

# Build a dict of {sheet_name: DataFrame}
if ext in {'xlsx', 'xls'}:
    xls = pd.ExcelFile(file_name)
    data_frames = {sheet: pd.read_excel(xls, sheet_name=sheet) for sheet in xls.sheet_names}
elif ext =='csv':
    df_csv = pd.read_csv(file_name)
    sheet_name = 'CSV Sheet'
    data_frames = {sheet_name: df_csv}
else:
    raise ValueError(f"Unsupported file extension ‘{ext}’.")

with open(f'datadescription.md', 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    f.write(f"# Data description for {file_name}\n\n") 

    if ext in {'xlsx', 'xls'}:
        sheet_count = len(data_frames)
        f.write(f"## Number of sheets: \n{sheet_count}\n\n")

    for sheet_name, df in data_frames.items():
        
        f.write(f"## Sheet name: {sheet_name}\n\n")
        
        # Column headings
        f.write("### Column Headings\n")
        f.write(", ".join([f"'{col}'" for col in df.columns]) + "\n\n")
        
        # First 10 rows
        f.write("### First 10 Rows\n")
        f.write(df.head(10).to_markdown() + "\n\n")
        
        # Bottom 10 rows
        f.write("### Bottom 10 Rows\n")
        f.write(df.tail(10).to_markdown() + "\n\n")
        
        # Describe function include='all'
        f.write("### Describe (include='all')\n")
        f.write(df.describe(include='all').to_markdown() + "\n\n")
        
        # Info function verbose=True
        f.write("### Info (verbose=True)\n")
        buffer = io.StringIO()
        df.info(buf=buffer, verbose=True)
        info_output = buffer.getvalue()
        f.write("```\n" + info_output + "\n```\n\n")
        
        # Shape function
        f.write("### Shape\n")
        f.write(str(df.shape) + "\n\n")
        
        # Text columns unique values (excluding numeric, dates, and columns with 'date' in name)
        text_columns = [col for col in df.columns if df[col].dtype == object and 'date' not in col.lower()]
        if text_columns:
            f.write("### Unique values in text columns (up to 100 values)\n")
            for col in text_columns:
                series = df[col]
                uniques = series.dropna()
                uniques = uniques[uniques != ''].unique().tolist()
                if (series == '').any():
                    uniques.append("<empty string>")
                if series.isna().any():
                    uniques.append("<NaN>")
                unique_vals_quoted = [f"'{str(val)}'" for val in uniques[:100]]
                f.write(f"#### Column: {col}\n")
                f.write(", ".join(unique_vals_quoted) + "\n\n")
```

<title Chart: WWTP Locations Map with Treatment Level>

Please create an interactive map showing the locations of wastewater treatment plants from the file HydroWASTE_v10_-_UTF8.csv with the following specifications:

- Use LAT_WWTP and LON_WWTP columns for the plant locations
- Color code the points by LEVEL (treatment level) - use distinct colors for 'Primary', 'Secondary', and 'Advanced'
- When a point is clicked, display a popup showing:
  - WWTP_NAME
  - COUNTRY
  - POP_SERVED (formatted with commas as thousands separator)
  - DESIGN_CAP (formatted with commas as thousands separator, show "N/A" if missing)
  - LEVEL (treatment level)
- Add a legend showing the color coding for treatment levels
- Use a reasonable marker size that allows points to be visible but not overcrowded
- Save the output as an interactive HTML file
- The chart should have no title (title will be shown separately)

<code_output_result_agent>:
Here is the chart you requested (chart filename is WWTP_Locations_Map_with_Treatment_Level.png):

. The html version of this chart is available at WWTP_Locations_Map_with_Treatment_Level.html. Additional information (stored in not applicable): No additional information provided. This chart was created from this code (stored in WWTP_Locations_Map_with_Treatment_Level.py): # filename: WWTP_Locations_Map_with_Treatment_Level.py import pandas as pd import plotly.express as px import numpy as np file_name = "HydroWASTE_v10_-_UTF8.csv" df = pd.read_csv(file_name) # Prepare data: clean DESIGN_CAP, POP_SERVED for display and handle missing DESIGN_CAP def format_num(x): if pd.isna(x): return "N/A" try: return f"{int(x):,}" except Exception: return "N/A" df['DESIGN_CAP_DISPLAY'] = df['DESIGN_CAP'].apply(format_num) df['POP_SERVED_DISPLAY'] = df['POP_SERVED'].apply(lambda x: f"{int(x):,}" if not pd.isna(x) else "N/A") # Popup info creation as html strings for hover data df['Popup'] = ( "WWTP: " + df['WWTP_NAME'].fillna("N/A") + "
" + "Country: " + df['COUNTRY'].fillna("N/A") + "
" + "Population served: " + df['POP_SERVED_DISPLAY'] + "
" + "Design capacity: " + df['DESIGN_CAP_DISPLAY'] + "
" + "Level: " + df['LEVEL'].fillna("N/A") ) # Choose color map for levels level_order = ['Primary', 'Secondary', 'Advanced'] color_discrete_map = { 'Primary': 'blue', 'Secondary': 'orange', 'Advanced': 'green' } # Filter to only rows with valid lat/lon and level in our defined levels df_map = df[ df['LAT_WWTP'].notna() & df['LON_WWTP'].notna() & df['LEVEL'].isin(level_order) ].copy() fig = px.scatter_mapbox( df_map, lat="LAT_WWTP", lon="LON_WWTP", color="LEVEL", color_discrete_map=color_discrete_map, category_orders={"LEVEL": level_order}, hover_name="WWTP_NAME", hover_data={ "COUNTRY": True, "POP_SERVED_DISPLAY": False, # we add in custom tooltip "DESIGN_CAP_DISPLAY": False, "LEVEL": False, "LAT_WWTP": False, "LON_WWTP": False, }, custom_data=['Popup'], size_max=7, zoom=1, height=600, width=1000, ) # Use open source style with plotly white base and black axis lines (though Mapbox has no axis lines) fig.update_layout( mapbox_style="open-street-map", margin={"r":0,"t":0,"l":0,"b":0}, font=dict(size=16), legend_title_text="Treatment Level", showlegend=True, hovermode='closest', ) # Override hover template to show our popup html on click fig.update_traces( marker=dict(size=7, opacity=0.7), hovertemplate=None, ) # Add click interaction with popups - plotly does not have native click-to-popup on mapbox, # so use customdata in hovertemplate for the info display instead: fig.update_traces( hovertemplate="%{customdata[0]}", ) # Save files base_filename = "WWTP_Locations_Map_with_Treatment_Level" fig.write_html(f"{base_filename}.html", include_plotlyjs='cdn') fig.write_image(f"{base_filename}.png", scale=1, width=1000, height=600) # Save CSV of plotted data with relevant fields out_csv = df_map[['WWTP_NAME', 'COUNTRY', 'POP_SERVED', 'DESIGN_CAP', 'LEVEL', 'LAT_WWTP', 'LON_WWTP']] out_csv.to_csv(f"{base_filename}.csv", index=False) # Print filenames to terminal print(f"{base_filename}.py") print(f"{base_filename}.html") print(f"{base_filename}.png") print(f"{base_filename}.csv") Let me know if you would like any changes or improvements. If the chart is ok, make a tool call to add_chart_and_comment.'

<title Table: Number of WWTPs by Country and Treatment Level>

Please create a table showing the number of wastewater treatment plants in each country categorized by treatment level, using the file HydroWASTE_v10_-_UTF8.csv. The table should have the following specifications:

- Use COUNTRY and LEVEL columns
- Create a pivot table with countries as rows and treatment levels (Primary, Secondary, Advanced) as columns
- Add a "Total" column showing the total number of plants per country
- Sort the table by the Total column in descending order
- Include all countries in the dataset
- Save the output as a CSV file
- Display the table in a clear, formatted manner with the top 50 countries shown

<code_output_result_agent>:
Here is the table you requested: 

Additional information (stored in not applicable):
No additional information provided.
This table was created from this code (stored in WWTP_Counts_by_Country_and_Level.py):
# filename: WWTP_Counts_by_Country_and_Level.py
import pandas as pd

# Load data
file_name = "HydroWASTE_v10_-_UTF8.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(file_name)

# Filter to rows with non-missing COUNTRY and LEVEL
df_filtered = df[df['COUNTRY'].notna() & df['LEVEL'].notna()]

# Define known levels for consistency and ordering
level_order = ['Primary', 'Secondary', 'Advanced']

# Create pivot table counting number of WWTPs by COUNTRY and LEVEL
pivot = pd.pivot_table(
    df_filtered,
    index='COUNTRY',
    columns='LEVEL',
    values='WASTE_ID',
    aggfunc='count',
    fill_value=0
)

# Ensure all expected LEVEL columns are present even if missing in data
for lvl in level_order:
    if lvl not in pivot.columns:
        pivot[lvl] = 0

# Reorder columns according to level_order
pivot = pivot[level_order]

# Add Total column
pivot['Total'] = pivot.sum(axis=1)

# Sort by Total descending
pivot_sorted = pivot.sort_values(by='Total', ascending=False)

# Save to CSV
out_filename = "WWTP_Counts_by_Country_and_Level.csv"
pivot_sorted.to_csv(out_filename)

# Display top 50 countries nicely formatted
print(pivot_sorted.head(50).to_string())

# Print output filename for record
print(out_filename)

Let me know if you would like any changes or improvements. If the table is ok, make a tool call to add_chart_and_comment.'

The **United States leads with 14,819 plants** (62% secondary, 38% advanced), followed by Germany (4,257, 88% advanced) and Italy (4,090, 54% advanced). Developing nations show concerning patterns: **China has 2,486 plants with zero advanced treatment**, while **Russia's 1,270 facilities are entirely secondary**. Brazil (2,820 plants) shows 94% secondary treatment. Countries like South Africa (964), India (816, 25% primary), and Malaysia (484) rely heavily on secondary treatment. The data highlights infrastructure gaps in Africa and Asia, where many countries have fewer than 50 plants. Advanced treatment dominates in Western Europe (Germany, Austria, Sweden, Denmark), reflecting EU Urban Wastewater Treatment Directive compliance, while most emerging economies lack advanced nutrient removal capabilities.", "code_filename": "WWTP_Counts_by_Country_and_Level.py", "data_filename": "WWTP_Counts_by_Country_and_Level.csv"}

<process_agent>:
<title Chart: Design Capacity vs Population Served by Treatment Level>

Please create a scatter plot showing the relationship between design capacity and population served, color-coded by treatment level, using the file HydroWASTE_v10_-_UTF8.csv. The chart should have the following specifications:

- X-axis: POP_SERVED (population served)
- Y-axis: DESIGN_CAP (design capacity)
- Color code points by LEVEL (Primary, Secondary, Advanced) using the same colors as the map (blue for Primary, orange for Secondary, green for Advanced)
- Use log scale for both axes to better display the wide range of values
- Only include data points where both POP_SERVED and DESIGN_CAP are available (not null)
- Add a diagonal reference line representing 1:1 ratio (where capacity equals population served)
- Include a legend showing treatment levels
- Use semi-transparent markers (alpha=0.6) to show overlapping points
- Label axes as "Population Served (persons)" and "Design Capacity (persons)"
- Add grid lines for readability
- Save as both PNG and HTML
- The chart should have no title (title will be shown separately)

<code_output_result_agent>:
Here is the chart you requested (chart filename is DesignCapacity_vs_PopulationServed_by_TreatmentLevel.png):

.
The html version of this chart is available at DesignCapacity_vs_PopulationServed_by_TreatmentLevel.html.

<dat d
This chart was created from this data (stored in DesignCapacity_vs_PopulationServed_by_TreatmentLevel.csv):
Data too large - not provided.>
Additional information (stored in not applicable):
No additional information provided.
This chart was created from this code (stored in DesignCapacity_vs_PopulationServed_by_TreatmentLevel.py):
# filename: DesignCapacity_vs_PopulationServed_by_TreatmentLevel.py
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import numpy as np

# Load data
file_name = "HydroWASTE_v10_-_UTF8.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(file_name)

# Filter data for required columns and drop rows with missing POP_SERVED or DESIGN_CAP or LEVEL
df_plot = df[['POP_SERVED', 'DESIGN_CAP', 'LEVEL']].dropna(subset=['POP_SERVED', 'DESIGN_CAP', 'LEVEL']).copy()

# Keep only accepted treatment levels
level_order = ['Primary', 'Secondary', 'Advanced']
df_plot = df_plot[df_plot['LEVEL'].isin(level_order)]

# Ensure values are positive for log scale
df_plot = df_plot[(df_plot['POP_SERVED'] > 0) & (df_plot['DESIGN_CAP'] > 0)]

# Define colors consistent with prior map
color_discrete_map = {
    'Primary': 'blue',
    'Secondary': 'orange',
    'Advanced': 'green'
}

# Create scatter plot with log scale axes
fig = px.scatter(
    df_plot,
    x='POP_SERVED',
    y='DESIGN_CAP',
    color='LEVEL',
    category_orders={"LEVEL": level_order},
    color_discrete_map=color_discrete_map,
    opacity=0.6,
    labels={
        'POP_SERVED': "Population Served (persons)",
        'DESIGN_CAP': "Design Capacity (persons)",
        'LEVEL': "Treatment Level"
    },
    height=600,
    width=1000,
)

fig.update_xaxes(type="log", showgrid=True, gridwidth=1, gridcolor='lightgrey')
fig.update_yaxes(type="log", showgrid=True, gridwidth=1, gridcolor='lightgrey')

# Add diagonal 1:1 reference line (capacity = population served)
min_val = min(df_plot['POP_SERVED'].min(), df_plot['DESIGN_CAP'].min())
max_val = max(df_plot['POP_SERVED'].max(), df_plot['DESIGN_CAP'].max())
line_values = np.logspace(np.log10(min_val), np.log10(max_val), num=500)

fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=line_values,
    y=line_values,
    mode='lines',
    line=dict(color='black', dash='dash'),
    name='1:1 Capacity = Population'
))

# Layout adjustments for clarity
fig.update_layout(
    font=dict(size=16),
    legend_title_text="Treatment Level",
    margin=dict(l=60, r=20, t=20, b=60),
    hovermode='closest',
)

# Save files
base_filename = "DesignCapacity_vs_PopulationServed_by_TreatmentLevel"
fig.write_html(f"{base_filename}.html", include_plotlyjs='cdn')
fig.write_image(f"{base_filename}.png", width=1000, height=600, scale=1)

# Save the data used in chart to CSV
df_plot.to_csv(f"{base_filename}.csv", index=False)

# Print filenames for terminal
print(f"{base_filename}.py")
print(f"{base_filename}.html")
print(f"{base_filename}.png")
print(f"{base_filename}.csv")

Let me know if you would like any changes or improvements. If the chart is ok, make a tool call to add_chart_and_comment.'
